description: A large natural elevation of earth's surface rising dramatically above surrounding land. 'Toremoa' names the great risings of the earth—ancient stone reaching toward sky, holding snow and source-waters, standing as witnesses across generations.
sound_symbolism: The word rises from earth to sky. 'To' begins with dental grounding, solid foundation. Then 're' reaches upward, the ascending slope. 'Mo' holds mass, the mountain's presence. Finally 'a' opens at the summit, the peak meeting sky.
grammatical_notes: Functions as a noun. Plural with 'lo': 'lo toremoa' (mountains). Takes the classifier 'themo' when counted. Distinguished from 'molira' (hill) by scale and permanence. Often sacred in traditional contexts. Used metaphorically for obstacles, aspirations, and enduring presence.

pillars.secular-buddhist-philosophy: Mountains teach aspiration and perspective—the climb reveals new vistas, and from the summit one sees the interconnection of all below.
pillars.pre-industrial-wisdom: Mountains were sacred in nearly all traditional cultures—homes of spirits, sources of waters, places where earth touches sky.
